critic_raw:
The methodology is thoughtful and clearly written, but contains several genuine methodological flaws worth naming precisely.

**Issue 1: Temporal leakage via stratified random split**

The methodology stratifies and randomly samples 70/15/15 across the full 18-month period, explicitly to stabilize malicious-message rates. But this means training examples can be drawn from month 17 while test examples come from month 3. The sequence model consumes temporal context features: thread history, sender-recipient interaction frequency (prior email counts from the training window), and domain consistency. When a test message from month 3 has training-set messages from month 16 in its thread, the 'prior messages in conversation' feature is computed using future information relative to that test message — but those future messages appear in the training set. More critically, if a sender appears in both train and test (randomly interleaved), the sequence model can memorize sender-specific patterns during training and exploit them at test time, while the baseline deliberately strips sender identity. The stated control — evaluating 'new senders unseen in training' — partially addresses this but only post hoc. It does not fix leakage for the majority of messages with known senders. Random temporal splits in sequence models are a known source of inflated evaluation, and the stated justification (class-rate stability) is a preference for measurement convenience that creates a more fundamental validity problem.

Evidence that would settle this: Compare performance on the randomly-split test set against a purely time-ordered held-out set (last N months). If the sequence model degrades significantly on the temporal holdout while the baseline does not, temporal leakage is the explanation for the gap.

**Issue 2: Asymmetric model capacity and optimization investment, presented as a baseline comparison**

The baseline is deliberately fixed: logistic regression, fixed regularization, fixed TF-IDF, no hyperparameter search. The sequence model undergoes grid-search tuning on the validation set and has learned embeddings, attention, and graph features. The framing describes this as 'measuring the added value of temporal and graph-aware modeling,' but the experimental design conflates modeling paradigm with modeling effort. Any improvement could be attributable to (a) the graph and temporal features, (b) the nonlinear neural architecture, (c) the learned text representations vs. fixed TF-IDF, or (d) the additional hyperparameter optimization. The ablation study removes graph features from the sequence model, but there is no ablation that asks: 'what does a well-tuned nonlinear model (e.g., gradient-boosted trees) achieve on the same features as the baseline?' Without that, the claimed isolation of representational gain from temporal/graph modeling is not demonstrated — it is asserted.

Evidence: Add a tuned gradient-boosted classifier receiving the same anonymized structured features as the baseline. If this intermediate baseline closes most of the gap, the gain is from model capacity and tuning, not from temporal or graph context.

**Issue 3: The sequence model's attention mechanism is evaluated only indirectly**

The multi-head attention layer is described as 'explicitly modeling which prior messages drive the classification,' and this is cited as a structural advantage. But attention weights in transformers/LSTMs are notoriously unreliable as interpretability signals, and the experiment does not measure whether the attention is doing what is claimed. Specifically: if the sequence model achieves gains, the confound analysis checks performance on isolated messages vs. long threads — but this only tests whether threads help, not whether the attention mechanism is selecting the causally relevant prior messages vs. attending to high-frequency superficial signals (sender domain, message length) that happen to correlate with maliciousness in the training period.

This is not a pure interpretability complaint — it matters for generalization. If the model is attending to superficial distributional features of threads from the training period rather than structural attack patterns, performance will degrade on novel attack campaigns that do not share those surface features. The post-hoc thread-length analysis cannot distinguish these.

Evidence: On the validation set, examine whether attention weight entropy correlates with model correctness on new-sender examples. High-entropy (diffuse) attention on novel senders that the model still classifies correctly suggests content features are doing the work; concentrated attention on specific thread positions that matches known attack structural patterns would support the structural hypothesis.

**Issue 4: Label construction conflates detection difficulty**

Labels come from SOC investigations, user reports, sandbox detonations, and post-compromise analysis. These are systematically biased toward attacks that were caught or reported. User-reported phishing is substantially over-represented in commodity phishing; sophisticated targeted attacks that evaded initial detection are under-represented unless they led to compromise. Post-compromise labels add some of the latter, but only after incident closure. The net effect is that the malicious class in training and test skews toward detectable-by-existing-means attacks. The sequence model may appear to generalize while actually fitting to the same detectable-by-existing-means subpopulation that the baseline also captures, just more efficiently. The hardest-to-detect attacks — the ones that matter most operationally — are absent from evaluation.

This cannot be fully resolved within this dataset. Evidence of the problem: measure performance separately on post-compromise-sourced labels (attacks that evaded prior detection) vs. user-report-sourced labels. A large performance gap between these subsets, with poor performance on post-compromise labels, indicates the evaluation is measuring catch-up detection, not prospective capability.

**Summary of verdict:**

Issue 1 (temporal leakage via random interleaved split) is a genuine methodological flaw that could directly inflate comparative results in favor of the sequence model. Issue 2 (conflated model capacity and feature advantage) undermines the central interpretive claim about what drives performance. Issues 3 and 4 are real but partly addressable within the stated framework. The combination of Issues 1 and 2 is sufficient to question the validity of the comparison as designed.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split over 18 months creates temporal leakage for sequence models: training messages from later periods appear in threads of test messages from earlier periods, and sender-level memorization is enabled for the sequence model but not the baseline.

The baseline is intentionally weakened (fixed regularization, no hyperparameter search, no nonlinear capacity) while the sequence model is fully tuned, conflating architectural paradigm with optimization effort and preventing isolation of the graph/temporal feature contribution.

The attention mechanism is assumed to learn structurally meaningful patterns, but no evaluation distinguishes structural attention from superficial distributional correlation, which has different generalization implications under novel campaigns.

Label construction is biased toward previously detected threats, systematically under-representing sophisticated attacks that evaded existing tools, so the evaluation may measure catch-up detection rather than prospective generalization.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split over 18 months creates temporal leakage for sequence models: training messages from later periods appear in threads of test messages from earlier periods, and sender-level memorization is enabled for the sequence model but not the baseline.

The baseline is intentionally weakened (fixed regularization, no hyperparameter search, no nonlinear capacity) while the sequence model is fully tuned, conflating architectural paradigm with optimization effort and preventing isolation of the graph/temporal feature contribution.

The attention mechanism is assumed to learn structurally meaningful patterns, but no evaluation distinguishes structural attention from superficial distributional correlation, which has different generalization implications under novel campaigns.

Label construction is biased toward previously detected threats, systematically under-representing sophisticated attacks that evaded existing tools, so the evaluation may measure catch-up detection rather than prospective generalization.
